sources.author: Morten Stender
quotes: Enterprise Architecture (EA) is no longer a discipline defined by static artefacts and quarterly governance reviews.

findings.bottom_line: Traditional EA governance operates on a slower cadence than AI deployment, producing decisions based on outdated context. Multiple sources describe EA evolving from periodic, manual reviews toward a continuously updated, AI-augmented function — though this depends on data quality and repository maturity most organisations have not yet achieved.
findings.body_html:
TOGAF's Architecture Development Method and similar governance frameworks were designed around phased, deliberative cycles: proposals reviewed days or weeks after submission, deliberated in committee, and approved — often based on information that has since changed. Six sources identify this mismatch as a barrier to AI adoption, with three specifically citing TOGAF's phased cycle model as misaligned with AI deployment velocity.

Two sources describe traditional EA as operating in open-loop mode — repositories intended as the source of truth become outdated artifacts between review cycles. Organisations with clean, modular architectures gain disproportionate advantage from this shift — two sources report 30–50% faster AI adoption — while legacy-burdened organisations face a compounding problem: they cannot adopt AI fast enough to modernise, and they cannot modernise fast enough to adopt AI.

Ten sources describe EA evolving from static modeling toward a living, continuously learning system with real-time feedback loops. Four sources describe AI enabling continuous updates to EA repositories, shifting from periodic reviews to near-real-time governance. Three sources describe AI agents that pre-check architectural proposals against standards, generate draft decisions, and surface relevant context at the point of decision — routing only exceptions to human architects. Five sources describe this as a fundamental shift in the EA function: from designing static structures to stewarding behavioral systems.

Sources describe this vision as technically feasible — harvesting agents that auto-discover architecture from deployment pipelines, dependency agents that map integration patterns, conformance agents that check deployments against standards. However, achieving it depends on data quality and repository maturity that most organisations have not yet reached.
findings.practitioner_text: Measure your governance cycle time — from architecture proposal submission to approved decision. Compare it against your deployment cadence. For the EA repository, identify the artifact that goes stale fastest and connect it to an automated data source as a first step toward continuous updates.

findings.bottom_line: Multiple sources report that specialized, task-specific models outperform larger general-purpose models on defined enterprise tasks. Because model capabilities and costs change rapidly, enterprise architecture must treat the model layer as swappable.
findings.body_html:
Four sources describe a shift away from defaulting to the largest available model. Specialized small language models fine-tuned for specific domains outperform general-purpose LLMs on targeted tasks at lower cost. Two sources state explicitly that model size does not correlate with business value, and that multi-component systems using task-specific models deliver better accuracy, cost efficiency, and performance.

Five sources observe that foundation models are commoditizing — performance differences between leading models on enterprise tasks are narrowing with each release cycle. The implication across these sources is consistent: selecting the right model for each task matters more than selecting the best model overall.

This has a direct architectural consequence. Six sources describe composable, modular architectures as essential for AI systems because AI components evolve at different rates. One source reports that rigid architectures became obsolete within months. Designing the model layer as interchangeable — supporting multiple model sizes and providers — is described as more durable than optimizing for a single frontier model.
findings.practitioner_text: Classify current AI use cases by task complexity. Evaluate whether a fine-tuned smaller model matches or exceeds your default frontier model on defined tasks, measuring accuracy, latency, and cost per inference.

findings.bottom_line: Eleven sources describe multi-agent systems as the emerging enterprise AI architecture. Multiple sources note that decomposition into specialized agents follows the same logic as microservices, but two sources identify qualitatively new failure modes when agents influence each other's outputs.
findings.body_html:
Eleven sources independently describe multi-agent systems — specialized agents collaborating through defined protocols — as the emerging architectural pattern for enterprise AI, replacing monolithic deployments. Sources describe orchestrator-worker hierarchies, meta-agents delegating to specialized workers, and multi-agent constellations with varying levels of autonomy.

Seven sources identify open standards (MCP, A2A) as essential for avoiding vendor lock-in in heterogeneous agent ecosystems. Three sources describe a semantic layer or enterprise knowledge graph as the shared context that enables agents to operate across domains. The architectural question multiple sources raise is not which protocol to adopt, but whether to abstract the protocol layer — since MCP and A2A are still evolving.

Two sources identify a risk profile distinct from traditional distributed systems. When agents influence each other through shared context, chained outputs, or collaborative decision-making, feedback loops and cascade failures become possible in ways that deterministic systems do not exhibit. Existing observability and risk frameworks were designed for deterministic systems and may not detect scenarios where one agent's inaccurate output becomes another agent's confident input.
findings.practitioner_text: Design AI platforms with an agent registry and protocol abstraction layer. Implement distributed tracing across agent interactions. Define blast-radius controls early — before agent-to-agent dependencies make failure modes difficult to isolate.

findings.body_html:
Six sources describe a consistent pattern: organisations take a human-designed workflow — sequential approval chains, handoff points between specialists, periodic review checkpoints — and add an AI agent to it. The agent inherits the workflow's constraints without leveraging its own strengths. Multiple sources advocate a "zero-based" approach: start with the desired outcome and design the process around agent capabilities rather than automating existing steps.

Four sources identify a prerequisite: processes must be well-defined and quantifiable before AI can be applied effectively. When workflows and decision rules are documented and maintained, AI can amplify what works. Without that baseline, two sources report that poorly designed agentic applications can add work to a process rather than reduce it.

Two sources from BCG describe AI transformation effort as following a 10/20/70 distribution: 10% algorithms, 20% technology backbone, 70% people and processes. Two sources describe POC fatigue — scattered AI initiatives diluting focus — and advocate narrowing from breadth to depth on a small number of high-impact use cases. One organisation reports narrowing over 800 AI use cases down to 20.
findings.practitioner_text: Before approving an AI agent deployment, assess whether the target process is documented with quantitative data, and whether the workflow has been redesigned for agent strengths or is being automated as-is.

Seven sources describe a consistent progression in AI value realization. Only 10% of organisations are reported to have mature FinOps governance. Two sources report AI budgets rising from 3% to 5% of annual business budgets between 2025 and 2026, with projections of up to half of technology spending directed toward AI in the long term.
findings.practitioner_text: Classify current AI investments by value type — productivity/cost savings versus revenue/growth. Assess whether the portfolio mix matches leadership expectations and whether the platform architecture built for phase-one use cases can support phase-two applications.

findings.bottom_line: Five sources describe AI agents as workforce participants rather than tools. Three sources identify a need for management frameworks that diverge from traditional ITSM. Eight sources establish governance and accountability as non-negotiable — but only two sources address who should own agent management, and their answer (business domains, not IT) is not yet broadly supported.
findings.body_html:
Five sources describe AI agents as a new category of workforce participant that organisations must integrate alongside human workers. One survey reports 76% of executives view agentic AI more as a coworker than a tool. This shift changes what management means: agents require onboarding (testing, validation, access provisioning), performance management (accuracy monitoring, cost tracking, drift detection), lifecycle management (versioning, retraining, retirement), and governance for when they produce unexpected outputs.

Three sources describe these requirements as diverging from traditional ITSM frameworks. AI agents are non-deterministic and context-dependent, producing failure modes that existing runbooks do not anticipate. Eight sources establish that governance, trust, and accountability for autonomous agent actions are non-negotiable for scalable enterprise adoption.

However, the question of who owns agent management remains largely unaddressed. Two sources propose distributing agent accountability to business domains — with central platform teams controlling core infrastructure while business units assemble, train, test, deploy, and monitor agents. One source recommends charging general managers with meeting AI targets rather than the CIO or CTO. But this model is not broadly supported across the literature, and an unresolved tension persists: if agents are workforce augmentation, existing management structures may apply; if agents are a fundamentally new category of autonomous labour, new organisational structures are needed.
findings.practitioner_text: Create an AI agent registry. For every deployed agent, document its role, capabilities, access permissions, data inputs, decision authority, performance metrics, and accountable human owner. Clarify whether agent ownership sits with business domains, IT, or a cross-functional structure.

findings.body_html:
Three sources report significant gaps between AI ambition and infrastructure capability. Only 22% of organisations have architectures that fully support AI workloads without modification. Only 23% can connect AI applications to relevant business data without changes. 87% of senior leaders report substantial barriers to adopting agentic AI.

Three sources describe AI infrastructure costs in 2025 as having caught organisations off guard. One source projects 5–10% of technology spending directed toward building foundational AI capabilities in the near term, with potentially 50% in the long term. Two sources report AI budgets rising from 3% to 5% of annual business budgets between 2025 and 2026.

Three sources observe that industry investment has focused disproportionately on AI models while the underlying infrastructure — compute, networking, storage, integration with enterprise systems — has received less attention. One source frames this as building the "brain" without building the "nervous system" that connects it to the enterprise.
findings.practitioner_text: Map each planned AI workload to its compute, network, storage, and integration requirements. Compare against current capacity to identify the infrastructure investment gap.

findings.bottom_line: Four sources describe operating models that are designed to evolve as fast as the technology they support. Two sources argue that making implicit business logic explicit and machine-readable is a prerequisite for effective AI agent deployment. Two sources advocate pragmatic, fit-for-purpose architecture over idealized designs.
findings.body_html:
Four sources describe a need for operating models built around adaptability — embedding an always-evolving mindset into structure, culture, and strategy. The argument across these sources is that organisations which learn as fast as the technology they adopt will outperform those that treat operating model changes as periodic events.

BCG articulates one version of this through their "Enterprise as Code" concept: capturing an organisation's implicit operating model — workflows, decision rules, governance logic — and expressing it as code that both people and AI systems can read and execute. BCG argues this shifts organisations from intuition-based operations to specification-based ones, and that AI transformation effort follows a 10/20/70 distribution (10% algorithms, 20% technology, 70% people and processes). Their "Freedom within a Frame" model proposes centralised infrastructure and governance with decentralised innovation by business units, reporting cost reductions of up to 30% and time-to-market improvements of 50%.

Two sources advocate pragmatic, fit-for-purpose architectural approaches over idealized visions. One source notes that enterprise realities — vendor lock-in, data silos, security requirements — prevent clean theoretical architectures from surviving contact with production. For smaller organisations, "just enough architecture" may be the appropriate stance.
findings.practitioner_text: Identify one high-volume business process and make its operating logic explicit and machine-readable — decision rules, exception paths, governance controls — in a format an AI agent could interpret. Use this to assess how much implicit knowledge needs to be codified before agents can operate effectively.

findings.bottom_line: Five sources describe AI sovereignty — control over data, models, and decision rights — as moving from an IT concern to a board-level priority. Seven sources identify rapidly evolving AI regulations as a driver that enterprise architectures must accommodate.
findings.body_html:
Five sources describe AI sovereignty as an increasingly strategic concern. Sources describe sovereignty-by-default approaches to data governance, sovereign cloud architectures driven by data sensitivity requirements, and growing awareness of the strategic importance of controlling the data that powers AI systems.

Seven sources identify evolving AI regulations — the EU AI Act, NIST AI RMF, GDPR, ISO/IEC 42001 — as requiring enterprise architectures that can adapt to new compliance requirements as they emerge. Where data resides, which models process it, who controls training data, and what jurisdictional rules apply — these constrain subsequent architecture decisions.

The sovereignty theme intersects with infrastructure readiness. Infrastructure must satisfy sovereignty constraints that limit where it can be deployed, which vendors can provide it, and what data can flow through it. Organisations operating across jurisdictions face additional architectural complexity that single-hyperscaler approaches may not accommodate.
findings.practitioner_text: Map current and planned AI workloads to their data residency, model provenance, and jurisdictional constraints. Where sovereignty requirements apply, assess whether current cloud and infrastructure architecture can satisfy them.
